tweets.title: R to @sidhant: English Translation: On July 22, 2026, Member of the Political Bureau of the CPC Central Committee and Foreign Minister Wang Yi met with Indian External Affairs Minister Subrahmanyam Jaishankar in Manila. Wang Yi stated that over the past two years, President Xi Jinping and Prime Minister Modi have successfully held meetings in Kazan and Tianjin. The two leaders agreed that China and India are partners, not competitors, and represent development opportunities for each other rather than threats. This has guided China-India relations onto a track of improvement and development. Through joint efforts by both sides, institutional exchanges between the two countries have gradually resumed, peace and tranquility have been maintained in the border areas, and bilateral trade volume has reached new highs. These achievements are hard-won and should be cherished. At a time when changes unseen in a century are accelerating and the world is entering a new period of turbulence and transformation, China and India, as representatives of emerging economies and important members of the Global South, should proceed from the well-being of humanity, demonstrate the responsibility of major countries, consolidate the positive momentum in bilateral relations, and strengthen coordination and cooperation on international and regional affairs. This will contribute positively to advancing the process of multipolarity and the democratization of international relations. China is ready to work with India to implement the important consensus reached by the two leaders, maintain close high-level exchanges, strengthen practical cooperation in areas such as economy and trade, media, and people-to-people exchanges, properly handle sensitive issues, and help people from all walks of life in both countries develop a correct mutual understanding. On the basis of peaceful coexistence, both sides should explore the right way for two neighboring major countries to treat each other with mutual respect, mutual trust, common development, and shared prosperity, and continuously inject new momentum into China-India relations. In the coming two years, India and China will successively assume the rotating presidency of BRICS. The two sides should strengthen coordination and cooperation to promote the development and growth of the “BRICS Plus” cooperation mechanism, better represent and safeguard the common interests of developing countries. China will continue to support India in successfully hosting this year’s BRICS Summit. Jaishankar stated that the two meetings between the Indian and Chinese leaders confirmed the positioning of the two countries as partners rather than rivals, charting the course for the development of bilateral relations. As important neighbors and populous nations, India-China relations carry regional and global significance. India’s positions on issues concerning Taiwan and Tibet remain unchanged. India respects China’s sovereignty and has always adopted a forward-looking attitude to advance a positive and cooperative bilateral relationship. India is willing to work with China to implement the consensus reached by the two leaders, strengthen high-level exchanges, make good use of bilateral mechanisms, advance practical cooperation in trade, people-to-people exchanges and other fields, maintain peace and stability in the border areas, and properly manage differences, so as to make continuous efforts to promote the improvement and development of bilateral relations. He thanked China for supporting India’s presidency of the BRICS Summit this year and expressed the hope to continue strengthening collaboration with China to jointly uphold multilateralism and the legitimate rights and interests of developing countries. The two sides also exchanged views on international and regional issues of common concern, including Iran and Myanmar.
tweets.content:
English Translation:

On July 22, 2026, Member of the Political Bureau of the CPC Central Committee and Foreign Minister Wang Yi met with Indian External Affairs Minister Subrahmanyam Jaishankar in Manila.

Wang Yi stated that over the past two years, President Xi Jinping and Prime Minister Modi have successfully held meetings in Kazan and Tianjin. The two leaders agreed that China and India are partners, not competitors, and represent development opportunities for each other rather than threats. This has guided China-India relations onto a track of improvement and development. Through joint efforts by both sides, institutional exchanges between the two countries have gradually resumed, peace and tranquility have been maintained in the border areas, and bilateral trade volume has reached new highs. These achievements are hard-won and should be cherished.

At a time when changes unseen in a century are accelerating and the world is entering a new period of turbulence and transformation, China and India, as representatives of emerging economies and important members of the Global South, should proceed from the well-being of humanity, demonstrate the responsibility of major countries, consolidate the positive momentum in bilateral relations, and strengthen coordination and cooperation on international and regional affairs. This will contribute positively to advancing the process of multipolarity and the democratization of international relations.

China is ready to work with India to implement the important consensus reached by the two leaders, maintain close high-level exchanges, strengthen practical cooperation in areas such as economy and trade, media, and people-to-people exchanges, properly handle sensitive issues, and help people from all walks of life in both countries develop a correct mutual understanding. On the basis of peaceful coexistence, both sides should explore the right way for two neighboring major countries to treat each other with mutual respect, mutual trust, common development, and shared prosperity, and continuously inject new momentum into China-India relations.

In the coming two years, India and China will successively assume the rotating presidency of BRICS. The two sides should strengthen coordination and cooperation to promote the development and growth of the “BRICS Plus” cooperation mechanism, better represent and safeguard the common interests of developing countries. China will continue to support India in successfully hosting this year’s BRICS Summit.

Jaishankar stated that the two meetings between the Indian and Chinese leaders confirmed the positioning of the two countries as partners rather than rivals, charting the course for the development of bilateral relations. As important neighbors and populous nations, India-China relations carry regional and global significance. India’s positions on issues concerning Taiwan and Tibet remain unchanged. India respects China’s sovereignty and has always adopted a forward-looking attitude to advance a positive and cooperative bilateral relationship. India is willing to work with China to implement the consensus reached by the two leaders, strengthen high-level exchanges, make good use of bilateral mechanisms, advance practical cooperation in trade, people-to-people exchanges and other fields, maintain peace and stability in the border areas, and properly manage differences, so as to make continuous efforts to promote the improvement and development of bilateral relations.

He thanked China for supporting India’s presidency of the BRICS Summit this year and expressed the hope to continue strengthening collaboration with China to jointly uphold multilateralism and the legitimate rights and interests of developing countries.

The two sides also exchanged views on international and regional issues of common concern, including Iran and Myanmar.

22nd Jul 2026:

20th Jul 2026:

- We will be posting daily war updates on the ongoing war in Middle East
- ⁠Follow us for daily Brief

● The United States executed its 10th consecutive night of airstrikes on Iran, targeting command centers, air defense systems, maritime capabilities, and missile and drone launch sites. The U.S. strikes are part of an ongoing effort to reopen the Strait of Hormuz and degrade Iranian military capabilities.

● U.S. President Donald Trump warned that the U.S. is prepared to respond if Houthi rebels in Yemen act on their threat to blockade commercial shipping in the Red Sea. He also indicated that the U.S. will soon strike the heavily fortified "Pickaxe Mountain" area, which is linked to Iran's nuclear program.

● Iran attacked a tanker in the Strait of Hormuz, forcing the crew to abandon the vessel. Furthermore, the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps claimed to have blown up two vessels attempting to transit the waterway.

● The Iranian army stated that it fired surface-to-surface missiles at U.S. HIMARS missile systems stationed at the Arifjan base in Kuwait. Kuwait's Ministry of Electricity reported that Iranian strikes also hit water desalination and power plants for a fourth consecutive day, sparking fires.

● Regional mediators, including Qatar, Egypt, and Pakistan, proposed a 10-day ceasefire cooling-off period to the U.S. and Iran. Meanwhile, Iran's interior minister traveled to Pakistan for talks regarding the collapsed U.S.-Iran memorandum of understanding.

● Jordan's military intercepted five Iranian drones and three ballistic missiles targeting the kingdom. Bahrain's military similarly reported shooting down multiple Iranian aerial aggressions.

● The Lebanese Armed Forces claimed the Israeli military opened fire near its troops as Lebanese soldiers began deploying into the Zawtar al-Gharbiyeh pilot zone in southern Lebanon. An Israeli military source stated the shots were fired because the Lebanese soldiers had crossed the established pilot zone borders.

tweets.content:
EAM Dr. S. Jaishankar met with Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi to review the gradual normalization of India-China relations since the October 2024 Kazan summit.

India’s stance remains firm: peace and tranquility in the border areas is the absolute prerequisite for normal ties.

EAM highlighted several concrete steps taken toward normalization over the past few months, including:
▪️ Resumption of direct flights between India and China
▪️ Restarting the Kailash Mansarovar Yatra
▪️ Recommencing bilateral border trade
▪️ Updating the visa regime

Despite the recent border de-escalation, Jaishankar clearly outlined the remaining structural issues that India expects to be addressed:
▪️ Ensuring fair market access for Indian goods
▪️ Correcting the ongoing trade imbalance
▪️ Securing predictability in supply chains

tweets.content:
BREAKING: Terrorists Attack J&K Police Party in Anantnag

Terrorists opened fire on a Jammu & Kashmir Police party at Lal Chowk, Anantnag, along the Kokernag–Anantnag Road, around 12:30 PM on July 22.

Head Constable Ashiq Hussain of the IRP 3rd Battalion, who was deployed for Amarnath Yatra security, sustained critical injuries and later succumbed at GMC Anantnag.

Police personnel returned fire, but the terrorists managed to flee the scene. Additional security forces have been rushed to the area, and a massive search operation is underway to trace the attackers.

tweets.title: Government Introduces 50% CAPF Quota for Ex-Agniveers The Government has informed Parliament that 50% of Constable (GD) and Rifleman vacancies in the CRPF, BSF, CISF, ITBP, SSB, and Assam Rifles will be reserved for Ex-Agniveers. Key provisions: • No written examination • Exemption from Physical Standards Test (PST) and Physical Efficiency Test (PET) • 3-year age relaxation for all Ex-Agniveers • 5-year one-time age relaxation for the first Agniveer batch • A dedicated Ex-Agniveer Wing has been created in the Ministry of Home Affairs. The first batch of Agniveers has not yet completed its four-year tenure, so the policy is yet to be implemented through its first recruitment cycle.
tweets.content:
Government Introduces 50% CAPF Quota for Ex-Agniveers

The Government has informed Parliament that 50% of Constable (GD) and Rifleman vacancies in the CRPF, BSF, CISF, ITBP, SSB, and Assam Rifles will be reserved for Ex-Agniveers.

Key provisions:
• No written examination
• Exemption from Physical Standards Test (PST) and Physical Efficiency Test (PET)
• 3-year age relaxation for all Ex-Agniveers
• 5-year one-time age relaxation for the first Agniveer batch
• A dedicated Ex-Agniveer Wing has been created in the Ministry of Home Affairs.

The first batch of Agniveers has not yet completed its four-year tenure, so the policy is yet to be implemented through its first recruitment cycle.

tweets.title: India Rules Out Return to Existing Indus Waters Treaty India has ruled out restoring the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) in its current form, with any future agreement contingent on Pakistan credibly and permanently ending cross-border terrorism. New Delhi is also accelerating five hydropower projects on the Chenab River with a combined capacity of 4,870 MW, aimed at strengthening water security, clean energy generation, and fuller utilisation of India's rights in the Indus basin. The projects include Sawalkote (1,856 MW), Pakal Dul (1,000 MW), Ratle (850 MW), Kiru (624 MW), and Kwar (540 MW). Four are under construction, while Sawalkote remains in the clearance stage.
tweets.content:

The projects include Sawalkote (1,856 MW), Pakal Dul (1,000 MW), Ratle (850 MW), Kiru (624 MW), and Kwar (540 MW). Four are under construction, while Sawalkote remains in the clearance stage.
